questions.questionText: Which of the following hormones is synthesized in the hypothalamus and released from the posterior pituitary?
questions.explanation: ADH (vasopressin) and oxytocin are made in the hypothalamus and stored in the posterior pituitary.

questions.questionText: Iodine is essential for the synthesis of hormones from the:
questions.explanation: T3 and T4 require dietary iodine for synthesis.

questions.questionText: The primary regulator of blood calcium levels, acting to increase serum calcium, is:
questions.explanation: PTH raises serum Ca2+ by bone resorption, renal reabsorption, and activating Vitamin D.

questions.questionText: Cortisol is secreted by which zone of the adrenal cortex?
The catecholamines (epinephrine and norepinephrine) are produced in the:
questions.explanation: Chromaffin cells in the adrenal medulla secrete catecholamines.

questions.questionText: Insulin is produced by which cells of the pancreatic islets?
questions.explanation: Beta cells produce insulin; alpha cells produce glucagon.

questions.questionText: Which hormone acts primarily to lower blood glucose levels?
questions.explanation: Insulin promotes glucose uptake into cells.

questions.questionText: Aldosterone secretion is primarily stimulated by:
questions.optionC: Angiotensin II and High serum Potassium
questions.explanation: Angiotensin II (due to low volume/BP) and hyperkalemia are the main secretagogues.

questions.questionText: Acromegaly in adults is caused by hypersecretion of:
questions.explanation: Excess GH after epiphyseal closure leads to acromegaly.

questions.questionText: Which hormone is responsible for the maintenance of the corpus luteum during early pregnancy?
questions.explanation: The developing placenta secretes hCG which mimics LH.

questions.questionText: In Endocrine System, which statement is most accurate regarding the core definition and diagnostic concept?
questions.optionA: It reflects the most exam-relevant definition used in standard Physiology texts
questions.optionB: It is an obsolete definition no longer used in clinical practice
questions.optionC: It is used only in pediatric population and never in adults
questions.optionD: It has no diagnostic significance in NEET-PG pattern questions
questions.explanation: This tests foundational definition-level knowledge from standard Physiology references for Endocrine System. Subject: Physiology. Chapter: Endocrine System.

questions.questionText: The most important etiology/risk factor to suspect first in a typical Endocrine System scenario is:
questions.optionA: The common high-yield risk factor repeatedly asked in entrance exams
questions.optionB: A rare cause seen only in isolated case reports
questions.optionD: An etiology excluded by routine first-line workup
questions.explanation: NEET-PG and INI-CET focus on common, high-yield causes before rare differentials. Subject: Physiology. Chapter: Endocrine System.
